title: Cat Vomiting Green Fluid: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat vomiting green liquid? Learn what bile means, when it becomes a medium-level emergency, and how to triage your pet's symptoms.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green vomit in cats is often caused by bile or plant ingestion and is typically a medium-urgency issue that requires veterinary attention if it persists or is accompanied by lethargy.

Why is my cat vomiting green fluid and what does it mean?

  • Green vomit is usually caused by bile, a digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder.
  • This color typically appears when a cat vomits on an empty stomach or when vomiting is severe enough to bring up contents from the small intestine.
  • In some cases, the green color may indicate that your cat has ingested grass or potentially toxic plant material.

How urgent is it if my cat is vomiting green fluid?

  • The urgency for green vomiting is generally considered Medium.
  • If your cat vomits once but continues to act normally, eat, and use the litter box, you should monitor them closely.
  • The situation becomes a higher priority if the vomiting persists, your cat becomes lethargic, or they refuse to eat for more than 24 hours.
  • Persistent vomiting can quickly lead to dangerous dehydration and electrolyte imbalances in felines.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's vomit to help the vet?

  • Taking a clear photo of the vomit allows the veterinary team to see the exact shade of green and the consistency (foamy, watery, or thick).
  • A photo helps identify the presence of any foreign objects, plant matter, or blood.
  • This visual evidence helps the vet determine if the issue is simple gastric irritation or a more serious concern like a gastrointestinal blockage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting in cats can result from dietary indiscretion, parasitic or bacterial/viral gastroenteritis, anxiety, or motion sickness. The vomitus may contain bile. Causes also include ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods or foreign material, drugs or toxins (antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism, or bacterial (Helicobacter) or viral (feline panleukopenia) infection. If vomiting persists, reevaluation is warranted, including abdominal palpation to check for foreign bodies or discomfort and careful oral examination for yarn, thread, or needles. Abdominal radiographs should be performed to look for radiopaque foreign objects or intestinal distention, indicating possible obstruction. Chronic vomiting can indicate underlying thyroid, liver, or kidney dysfunction and should be investigated.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Is green bile in cats always an emergency?
faqs.answer: Not always. A single episode may just mean an empty stomach. However, it becomes an emergency if it is accompanied by hiding, pain, or repeated retching.

faqs.question: Should I withhold food if my cat is vomiting green fluid?
faqs.answer: You can try withholding food for 2 to 4 hours to let the stomach settle, but never withhold water. If vomiting continues after reintroducing a small amount of food, call your vet.

faqs.question: Can hairballs cause green vomit?
faqs.answer: Yes, if a hairball is irritating the stomach lining or causing a partial blockage, the cat may vomit up bile-stained fluid along with or instead of the hairball itself.
